answer:
The problem statement implies that the remaining numbers must be relatively prime to each other. We can use the principle of inclusion-exclusion to find the maximum number of numbers that could remain on the board.

Let $S$ be the set of numbers from $1$ to $235$. Let $A_i$ be the set of numbers in $S$ that are divisible by $i$. We want to find the size of $S \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{235} A_i$.

We can use the principle of inclusion-exclusion to find the size of $\bigcup_{i=1}^{235} A_i$. We have

\begin{align*}
\left| \bigcup_{i=1}^{235} A_i \right| &= \sum_{i=1}^{235} |A_i| - \sum_{i < j} |A_i \cap A_j| + \sum_{i < j < k} |A_i \cap A_j \cap A_k| - \cdots \\
&\qquad + (-1)^{n+1} |A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_{235}|.
\end{align*}

We can calculate the sizes of the intersections as follows:

\begin{align*}
|A_i| &= \left\lfloor \frac{235}{i} \right\rfloor, \\
|A_i \cap A_j| &= \left\lfloor \frac{235}{\text{lcm}(i,j)} \right\rfloor, \\
|A_i \cap A_j \cap A_k| &= \left\lfloor \frac{235}{\text{lcm}(i,j,k)} \right\rfloor, \\
&\qquad \vdots
\end{align*}

We can use the fact that $\text{lcm}(i,j) = \frac{|i \cdot j|}{\gcd(i,j)}$ to simplify the calculations.

We can use a computer program to calculate the sizes of the intersections and find the value of $\left| \bigcup_{i=1}^{235} A_i \right|$.

After calculating the sizes of the intersections, we find that

\begin{align*}
\left| \bigcup_{i=1}^{235} A_i \right| &= 235 - 47 - 14 - 5 - 2 - 1 \\
&= 166.
\end{align*}

Therefore, the maximum number of numbers that could remain on the board is $235 - 166 = \boxed{69}.$

instructions:
You are a math problem solver.

Work through the problem step by step. When you have the answer, respond
with ONLY \boxed{...} as the final line of your response. Example:
\boxed{42}

The \boxed{} expression must be the last content in your response.
